title: Breaking bad news — new lung cancer diagnosis
candidateInstructions: You are an FY2 doctor in the respiratory outpatient clinic. Mr Thomas Griffiths, a 64-year-old retired carpenter, had a CT chest 2 weeks ago following an abnormal CXR. The CT confirms a 5cm right upper lobe mass consistent with primary lung carcinoma. Staging is currently being arranged. He has attended today for his results. Please break this news using appropriate communication skills.
examinerNotes: Lung cancer breaking bad news station. Candidate should use SPIKES framework. Key errors to penalise: using jargon, giving false hope, not using the word 'cancer', not allowing silence, giving too much information at once. Warning shot essential before giving news.

patient.name: Thomas Griffiths
patient.reasonForAttendance: I'm here to find out what the scan showed. I've been worried sick.

examination.general: Well-dressed man, visibly anxious, sitting on edge of chair

patient.emotionalState: Extremely anxious. Half-suspects the news is bad. Will become emotional and tearful when told. May initially be in shock and struggle to process information. Will ask 'Is it cancer?' directly. May become angry briefly, then tearful. Will ask about prognosis and whether it's curable.
patient.communicationStyle: Direct questions. Will push for specific answers. May become upset and need time. Partner called Joan waiting outside.
hiddenConcerns: My father died from lung cancer — is this the same?
Am I going to die from this?
Will I be able to see my grandchildren grow up?
Should I have come sooner when I first started coughing?
doNotVolunteer: Do not volunteer family history unless asked
Do not ask about prognosis unless doctor says it is cancer — then will ask directly

primaryDiagnosis: Primary Lung Carcinoma (right upper lobe, staging pending)

dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Mentions/checks private room, no interruptions, turns off pager
'What do you understand about why you had the scan?' or similar
'Are you happy for me to share the results with you today?'
Offers to bring Joan in before giving news

clinicalManagement.criterion: Gives warning shot before bad news
clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: 'I'm afraid the news is not what we hoped for' or equivalent

clinicalManagement.criterion: Gives diagnosis clearly and honestly — uses the word 'cancer'
clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: States 'cancer' or 'tumour' — does not avoid the word when asked directly
Outlines: staging scans, MDT meeting, referral to oncology/thoracic surgery
Does not give survival statistics without patient asking; appropriate response to prognosis question

interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Allows silence after delivering news, acknowledges emotions
Pauses to check comprehension, uses simple language

interpersonalSkills.criterion: Safety nets — follow-up, written information, support
interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Offers written information, follow-up appointment, Macmillan nurse contact

references.source: Baile WF et al. SPIKES — A six-step protocol for delivering bad news. Oncologist 2000

learningObjectives: Apply SPIKES framework for breaking bad news
Use appropriate warning shots before disclosing diagnosis
Handle direct questions about prognosis appropriately
